hook: Largest Romanesque church standing, with a crypt stacked full of relics
facts: Built from the 11th century over the tomb of Saturnin, Toulouse's first bishop, martyred around 250 by being dragged by a bull
Crypt holds one of the largest relic collections in Europe, including bodies said by tradition to be the apostles Simon and Jude
UNESCO-listed in 1998 as a stop on the Routes of Santiago de Compostela
Interior runs about 115 metres, vast for a Romanesque building
legend: Legend says Saturnin was tied to a bull and dragged down the hill until the rope broke, marking where the church now stands
w: A bishop tied to a bull and dragged down the hill, the rope snapping where the brick now rises. Pilgrims still file past on the Compostela road, but the real hoard waits below: a crypt crammed with reliquaries, bones tradition assigns to apostles Simon and Jude among them. Romanesque, immense, half ossuary.

hook: Dominican church whose single column fans into a stone palm tree
facts: Mother church of the Dominican Order, begun 1229 in southern Gothic brick to combat the Cathar heresy
A single central column throws out 22 ribs across the choir vault, nicknamed the palmier des Jacobins
Bones of Thomas Aquinas rest beneath the altar, transferred from Italy by papal decree in 1368
Built of pink Toulouse brick, the church split into a single nave and an aisle by its row of pillars
w: Stand under the last pillar and look up: 22 ribs spray outward like the fronds of a stone palm, holding the whole choir aloft on one column. The friars who built it hunted heretics; their greatest mind, Thomas Aquinas, lies under the altar, shipped here from Italy in 1368. Pink brick, engineering as theatre.

hook: Medieval mill dam often called the world's first shareholding company
facts: Dam and floating mills built across the Garonne from 1190 with the count's permission, cited by Rabelais as the most powerful mills in the world
From about 1250 owners traded shares whose value rose and fell with the mills' profits, sometimes called the earliest joint-stock company; it survived until 1946
Now a working EDF hydroelectric plant with a free art gallery in the old machine hall
A fish ladder with an underwater viewing window lets you watch salmon climb the weir
w: Eight centuries back, locals threw a dam across the Garonne and split the profits into tradable shares that rose and fell with the river, a stock market in flour long before stock markets. The mills ground on until 1946. Now turbines hum, art hangs in the old hall, and salmon climb a glass-walled ladder.

hook: An 1822 brick water tower turned one of the world's oldest photo galleries
facts: Designed 1822 by architect Jean-Antoine Raynaud as a pumping station beside the Pont Neuf, its machinery built by the city's cannon foundry director
Saved from demolition and opened in 1974 by Toulouse photographer Jean Dieuzaide as France's first public gallery devoted only to photography
Holds an important photo collection and one of France's main specialist photography libraries
Sits at the foot of the Pont Neuf on the Garonne's left bank
w: A round brick water tower from 1822, machinery cast by the cannon foundry, pumping the city's drinking supply. When demolition loomed, photographer Jean Dieuzaide grabbed it in 1974 and hung pictures inside, one of the first galleries anywhere devoted purely to the camera. Plumbing reborn as darkroom.

hook: Modern art in a 19th-century slaughterhouse, hiding a giant Picasso curtain
facts: Housed in slaughterhouses designed by Urbain Vitry, reopened as a museum and regional contemporary art fund in 2000
Owns Picasso's 1936 stage curtain La Depouille du Minotaure en costume d'Arlequin, about 8 by 13 metres
Painted for Romain Rolland's play Le 14 Juillet, given to Toulouse in 1965
So fragile it is shown only about six months a year in a purpose-built basement theatre
w: They killed cattle here until the trade moved on; the brick sheds now hold modern art. Down in a custom-built basement hangs Picasso's vast 1936 curtain, a flayed Minotaur in harlequin motley, painted for a Popular Front play. Too frail to show for long, it surfaces only half the year. Knives out, paint in.

hook: A pastel merchant's Renaissance palace, built on the blue that gilded Toulouse
facts: Built from 1555 for Pierre d'Assezat, one of the richest woad (pastel) merchants, by architect Nicolas Bachelier
The blue dye from the woad plant made Toulouse merchants fabulously wealthy in the 16th century
Among the first works of French classicism, its courtyard mixing brick with rare carved stone
Since 1994 it has housed the Bemberg Foundation, the private collection of Argentine Georges Bemberg
w: The blue pigment squeezed from the woad plant made a handful of Toulouse merchants obscenely rich, and Pierre d'Assezat spent his fortune on this courtyard in 1555: tiers of carved stone climbing over brick, classicism arriving early in the south. Today it shelters one collector's hoard of paintings. Built on dye.

hook: A medieval cloister where salvaged gargoyles stand at eye level in a row
facts: Fine arts museum in a Gothic Augustinian convent begun 1309, opened as a museum in 1801
The cloister was replanted as a medieval-style garden in 1995
Along its south side stands a line of gargoyles brought down from cathedral roofs to be seen close up
Holds one of Europe's largest sets of Romanesque carved capitals from La Daurade, Saint-Sernin and the cathedral
w: Gargoyles are meant to leer down from a roofline, unseen up close. In this convent cloister, replanted as a medieval garden, a row of them was hauled down to ground level, so you meet the grotesques eye to eye, mouths agape, paint flaking. Around them, one of Europe's great hoards of Romanesque capitals.

hook: A space park on the eastern edge with a real Russian Mir module to crawl through
facts: Opened June 1997 on the city's eastern outskirts as a space-themed science park
A full-scale 55-metre Ariane 5 stands outside alongside Soyuz and Mir mock-ups
The Mir module was shipped from Russia; visitors stoop through the tube where cosmonauts lived
Toulouse is France's aerospace capital, home to Airbus and CNES
w: Out on the eastern fringe a 55-metre Ariane 5 looms over the car park. Inside, you can stoop through an actual Mir module, freighted in from Russia, the same cramped tube where cosmonauts floated for months. Fitting for a city that builds Airbuses and runs France's space agency. Rockets in the suburbs.

hook: A hangar of giant walking machines, ruled by a 14-metre rideable Minotaur
facts: Sits on the old Montaudran airfield, where Aeropostale mail pilots once took off for Africa and South America
Asterion the Minotaur stands about 14 metres tall and carries passengers on its back through the streets
Home to roughly 200 strange theatrical machines built by the company La Machine
The same workshop tradition produced the giant marionettes that have paraded through Toulouse
w: On the runway where Aeropostale once launched mail planes toward the Andes, a hangar now houses 200 absurd machines and one colossus: Asterion, a 14-metre Minotaur of steel and timber that lumbers down the avenue with passengers riding its back. Half theatre, half nightmare. The myth, motorised.

hook: A three-way canal junction watched over by a marble allegory of the waterways
facts: Where the Canal du Midi (1681), Canal de Brienne (1770s) and Canal lateral a la Garonne meet, a fluvial crossroads
Twin bridges built 1771 to 1774 by engineer Joseph-Marie de Saget give the spot its name
Between them a 25-metre Carrara marble bas-relief by Francois Lucas (1773 to 1775) personifies the canal and the Garonne
Part of the Canal du Midi, UNESCO-listed in 1996
w: Three canals from three centuries braid together here under a pair of 1770s bridges. Wedged between them, a 25-metre slab of Carrara marble carves the Canal du Midi and the Garonne into reclining gods, the engineers' propaganda in stone. Boats still slide through the meeting of the waters. A quiet crossroads.

hook: A hillside necropolis entered through a neo-Egyptian gate of brick obelisks
facts: Opened 1840 after the mayor ordered the right-bank Catholic cemeteries closed and the graves moved here
The monumental neo-Egyptian portal, inaugurated July 1840, was designed by city architect Urbain Vitry
Two brick obelisks and colonnaded pavilions echo drawings from Napoleon's Egyptian campaign by Vivant Denon
The entrance was listed as a historic monument in 2019
w: When the old churchyards filled, the city marched its dead up the hill to a new ground in 1840 and gave it a startling door: twin brick obelisks and colonnaded pavilions, Egyptian revival cribbed from Napoleon's Nile expedition. Pass between the obelisks and the funerary avenues climb away. Death, dressed as a pharaoh.

hook: A plain street door hiding ceilings often called Toulouse's Sistine Chapel
facts: First stone laid 1622 by Louis XIII and Anne of Austria; the king reneged on funding it
Completed thanks to a parliament president whose five daughters were all Carmelite nuns
Ceilings painted in the late 17th century by Jean-Pierre Rivalz, inspired by the Sistine Chapel
Jean-Baptiste Despax finished them in the 18th century, including an Apotheosis of Saint Teresa of Avila
w: From rue de Perigord it reads as a blank wall. Step in and the whole barrel ceiling erupts in painted saints and skies, the work of Rivalz and then Despax, who looked to the Sistine and aimed higher. The king who laid the first stone in 1622 never paid up; five Carmelite sisters' father finished the job.

hook: Renaissance mansion of the man who bankrolled a captured king's ransom
facts: Built around 1530 for woad merchant Jean de Bernuy, of Spanish origin, who fled the Inquisition
Bernuy stood as chief guarantor for the ransom of Francis I after his capture at Pavia
Its tall stair tower, pierced by corner windows, ranks among the highest in the old city
Housing schools since 1567, it is now the Lycee Pierre-de-Fermat
w: Jean de Bernuy fled the Spanish Inquisition, grew rich on Toulouse blue, and was wealthy enough to guarantee the ransom of King Francis I after Pavia. His 1530 mansion flaunts it: a stair tower bristling with corner windows, one of the tallest in town. Schoolchildren have filled its courts since 1567. Old money, still standing.

hook: A copper dome over a plague hospital, the river's most photographed silhouette
facts: Hospital begun in the 12th century on the gravel bank (la grave) of the Garonne, outside the walls to isolate plague victims
Built between 1758 and 1845, the round Chapelle Saint-Joseph carries a copper-clad dome and lantern
The dome rises about 85 metres and is among the most photographed sights in Toulouse
The chapel has been a listed monument since 1978; the complex is now a health and culture site
w: Plague victims were once shipped here, to the gravel bank beyond the walls, where the sick, the orphaned and the outcast were kept apart. The hospital crowned itself with a copper dome 85 metres up, and at dusk it becomes the postcard shot of the Garonne, brick and water and that green cupola. Quarantine, gone glorious.

hook: France's grand provincial natural history hoard, beside a botanical garden
facts: Founded 1796 by naturalist Picot de Lapeyrouse, first in a former Carmelite monastery
Opened in its present building in 1865 under director Edouard Filhol
Holds more than 2.5 million specimens across some 3,000 square metres of galleries
Stands beside the Jardin des Plantes, laid out in 1794 on the grounds of the old Carmelite convent
w: One naturalist's cabinet of curiosities, started in 1796, swelled into 2.5 million specimens: skeletons, beetles, meteorites, mummies. It opened to the public here in 1865 and spills out into the Jardin des Plantes next door, itself planted on a dissolved Carmelite convent. Stuffed, pinned and labelled, a city's wonder cabinet.

hook: An iron market hall topped by tiny restaurants cooking the stalls below
facts: The square began in 1827 as a wood and feed market, renamed for Victor Hugo in 1886
Rebuilt as a covered iron hall in 1892, once packed with hundreds of stalls
Around 80 stallholders sell fish, tripe, cheese, charcuterie and regional produce, closed Mondays
Five small restaurants on the upper floor cook only produce bought from the market beneath them
w: Under an 1892 iron roof, fishmongers and tripe-sellers and cheese men shout their wares, much as they have for over a century. The trick lies overhead: a handful of cramped first-floor restaurants that cook nothing but what was bought downstairs that morning. Climb the stairs at noon and eat the market. No Mondays.

hook: Headquarters of the Occitan language, in a capitoul's 15th-century mansion
facts: Cultural centre for the Occitan language and culture, opened 2006 at 11 rue Malcousinat
Set in the Hotel de Boysson, a 15th-century mansion of the city magistrates (capitouls)
Holds the Tuta d'Oc bookshop, a radio studio and Occitan classes, run by the Convergencia Occitana association
Anchors the Occitania festival each September
w: The langue d'oc, tongue of the troubadours, never quite died here. Behind a 15th-century magistrate's facade on rue Malcousinat, it gets a clubhouse: Occitan lessons, a radio booth, a bookshop called the Tuta d'Oc, concerts in the old courtyard. Toulouse spelled Tolosa, and proud of it. A language, holding its ground.
